project_overview.project_name: Enhancing Sierra Leone Energy Access Project
project_overview.project_development_objective: To increase electricity access in Sierra Leone
project_overview.total_financing: US$52.7 million (IDA: US$50 million equivalent in SDR 35 million; Japan PHRD: US$2.7 million)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Approximately 276,000 people (43,000 households) with grid electricity; 18,000 people (3,000 households) with solar electricity; 200 health facilities; 500 schools; 9,100 industrial and commercial entities; women-led enterprises; off-grid solar companies

geographic_scope.coverage_type: National with focus on district headquarter towns and surrounding communities
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: Yes - Sierra Leone is classified as a fragile state with ongoing political fragility risks. The country experienced disputed elections requiring ECOWAS intervention in 2018, and almost every by-election since has been marred by violence. General election scheduled for early 2023 during project implementation. Pre-existing vulnerabilities include youth demographic with low education, power asymmetry, elite capture, and limited institutional capacity.

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Electrification of towns and communities through grid extension
key_components.description: Construction of three segments of sub-transmission and distribution lines (33 kV and 66 kV) totaling approximately 160 km to connect distribution networks in Kabala, Kailahun, and Pujehun to the 225 kV CLSG transmission line. Expansion of distribution networks in Waterloo, Makeni, Magburaka, and Koidu including 11 kV distribution lines, transformers, low voltage lines, service connections, and meters. Expected to provide electricity to approximately 38,300 residential customers and 8,300 industrial/commercial/public institutions in expansion areas, plus benefit 4,700 residential customers and 950 industrial/commercial customers through reliable grid connectivity.

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Electrification through mini-grids and standalone solar systems
key_components.description: Subcomponent 2(a): Installation of solar PV mini-grids with battery storage in Moyamba (600 kWp initially with 1,800 kWh battery storage) and 8-12 mini-grids in selected large communities (total 4 MW capacity). Subcomponent 2(b): Installation of solar PV systems for approximately 200 health facilities and 500 primary/secondary schools (total 2 MW capacity) with battery storage and water pump support. Subcomponent 2(c): Technical advisory services for market assessment, capacity building, awareness raising, and M&E.
key_components.financing: US$12.7 million (US$10.0 million IDA + US$2.7 million Japan PHRD)

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Human capital development and project implementation support
key_components.description: Subcomponent 3(a): Support to MoE for mini-grid operation/maintenance services procurement, clean cooking investment project preparation, institutional capacity strengthening for renewable energy promotion, gender disparity reduction in energy sector employment, and operational costs. Subcomponent 3(b): Support to EDSA for staff capacity improvement, governance strengthening, network climate resilience, and environmental/social management capacity.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Energy (MoE) for Components 2 and Subcomponent 3(a); Electricity Distribution and Supply Authority (EDSA) for Component 1 and Subcomponent 3(b); Ministry of Finance (MoF) as co-implementing agency
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: MoE has overall responsibility for project coordination, monitoring and reporting. EDSA reports progress on Component 1. Coordination with Ministry of Health (MoH) and Ministry of Education (MoED) for health facility and school electrification. Donor coordination through energy sector coordination group. Project Management Unit (PMU) at MoE and Project Management Team (PMT) at EDSA with support from international and local consultants.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF (July 1, 2016, revised November 2017 and August 2018). Open International Competitive Bidding (ICB) for contracts above US$10 million for works and US$2 million for goods. National Competitive Bidding for smaller contracts. Use of World Bank Standard Procurement Documents. STEP system for procurement management. Prior review for major contracts; post review for others.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: MoE responsible for overall M&E with support from EDSA. Quarterly and annual monitoring of PDO and intermediate results indicators. EDSA monitors key performance indicators and prepares quarterly/monthly reports. Project includes grievance redress mechanism with digital platform and GBV service provider. Annual stakeholder surveys planned. M&E system strengthening supported under TA component.

identified_risks.risk_description: Country's political environment remains fragile with disputed elections requiring ECOWAS intervention in 2018. General election scheduled for early 2023 during project implementation. Risk of political interference in project implementation and governance constraints.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Minimize Government's direct interference in project implementation. Ensure strict adherence to IDA procurement regulations. Implementation primarily through national utility supported by independent consultants. Strong focus on transparency and accountability.

identified_risks.risk_description: Country faces substantial macroeconomic risks with continuing domestic and external imbalances. COVID-19 pandemic expected to significantly disrupt economic activity and reduce growth. Large shock to GDP growth expected to reduce revenues while expenditures increase, creating significant financing gap.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Minimize requirement of government funding support to project. Ensure dedicated use of grant proceeds. Strong focus on improving EDSA's financial viability through management improvement and distribution loss reduction. Project designed to improve sector financial sustainability.

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Capacity in both EDSA and MoE remains weak despite some enhancement under previous IDA projects. Limited technical, operational, FM, and E&S risk management capacity. Weak staff capacity at all levels.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Intensified and comprehensive training and capacity building for MoE and EDSA staff. Continued engagement of international specialists in various fields. Recruitment of experienced consultants for procurement, FM, and safeguards. Young professionals being recruited to augment staff capacity. Component 3 includes significant capacity building activities.

identified_risks.risk_description: FM system and capacity in EDSA assessed as inadequate. Procurement capacity to implement World Bank Procurement Regulation not strong. Risk of delays in FM reporting and procurement processes.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Experienced international procurement specialist employed to assist. Intensive training and capacity building for EDSA and MoE on FM systems and new Procurement Regulation. Independent project FM unit with officers paid by project. Independent external audit annually. Regular FM reviews by World Bank team. Internal auditor to be hired and paid by project.

identified_risks.risk_description: Weak institutional capacity of EDSA for E&S management. Potential impacts include vegetation clearance, erosion, water pollution, dust and noise, land acquisition and displacement, battery disposal, worksite hazards, and community health/safety issues. GBV risk assessed as low but contextual GBV risk in Sierra Leone is high.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Framework instruments (ESMF, RPF, SEP, ESCP) prepared and disclosed. Site-specific ESIAs/ESMPs to be prepared during implementation. EDSA to complete recruitment of social and gender specialist. Integrated E&S information management system to be financed. Targeted E&S training for staff and contractors. Digital GRM platform with GBV service provider. Adequate budget for RAP implementation. SEA/SH risk mitigation measures commensurate with Moderate risk level.

identified_risks.risk_description: Pandemic has disrupted grid rollout construction works and supply chains for off-grid equipment from China. Vulnerable to country developments. Socio-economic impact may severely affect customer contributions to grid and off-grid. Component 3 activities may be disrupted by lockdowns.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Maximize use of online procurement platform for goods, works and consultant services. Front-loading of preparatory works to expedite implementation readiness. Project design adjusted to echo World Bank's COVID response. Support for health facilities and schools electrification particularly important in wake of COVID-19.

identified_risks.risk_description: Key risks include inundation and extreme temperature. Sierra Leone has second most intensive rainfalls in Africa with risks of landslides, coastal flooding and erosion. Extreme weather events and sea-level rise threaten critical infrastructure. High dependence on hydropower (39.7% of installed capacity) vulnerable to rainfall variability.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Design of lines, transformers and solar PV panels to consider risks of flooding, storm, wind and extreme temperature. Detailed hazard and risk assessments conducted in Freetown, Makeni, and Bo with GFDRR support. Local engineering studies to include assessment of landslide and flood impacts with mitigation measures. Integration of climate risks in engineering/feasibility studies following World Bank Good Practice Note. Potential measures include elevating assets, protective walls, high-elevation site selection.

triggered_safeguard_policies: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (ESS1)
Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (ESS3)
Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement (ESS5)
Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources (ESS6)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Project-specific GRM being set up to handle complaints and issues. GRM designed to collect, collate, review and redress stakeholders' concerns. Dedicated communication materials (GRM brochure/pamphlet) to be developed. GRM accessible and understandable for all stakeholders throughout project life. Applicable for contractors during construction and operations. Digital platform for GRM to be instituted with GBV service provider contracted for confidential uptake and response. Expected to be operational by project effectiveness. Also accessible through World Bank's corporate GRS and Inspection Panel.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Gender gaps identified: (1) In urban areas, poorer female-headed households have disproportionately lower access to energy; (2) Due to lower asset ownership and access to finance, women launch income-generating activities less often than men; (3) Women underrepresented in energy sector employment; (4) Women's basic needs in health and education lag behind men. Project addresses gaps through: targeted electrification of female-headed households; community awareness campaigns; capacity building for women-led businesses; electrification of health facilities with maternity wards; employment opportunities for women in energy sector; gender-focused institutional capacity building; women's participation in decision-making. Target to increase female staff at EDSA from 9% to 15%. Indicators track female access and female-headed business electrification.

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project designed with conflict sensitivity considerations. Addresses key fragility drivers including: (1) Economic diversification through energy access to support job creation and reduce youth frustration; (2) Infrastructure development to support economic recovery and poverty reduction; (3) Inclusive electrification targeting vulnerable populations including female-headed households; (4) Community engagement and stakeholder consultation to build social cohesion; (5) Transparent procurement and governance to reduce corruption and elite capture; (6) Support for public institutions (health, education) to improve service delivery and build state legitimacy. Project supports government priorities on doubling energy access and EDSA financial turnaround, contributing to state capacity and legitimacy.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Sierra Leone classified as fragile state with multiple fragility factors: (1) Political fragility - disputed elections, ECOWAS intervention required in 2018, ethno-regional divide, general election in early 2023; (2) Economic fragility - undiversified economy dependent on natural resources and subsistence agriculture, low productivity, high informality, limited job creation; (3) Institutional fragility - weak governance, corruption, coordination failures across MDAs, low institutional capacity; (4) Social fragility - youth bulge (75% under 35) with low skills and unmet expectations, high gender inequality, limited human capital; (5) Environmental fragility - susceptible to natural disasters and climate change, high rainfall intensity, deforestation, informal settlements on floodplains; (6) Health fragility - fifth lowest life expectancy globally (51 years), high maternal mortality (1,360 per 100,000), COVID-19 pandemic impacts. Project addresses fragility through infrastructure development, job creation, institutional capacity building, and service delivery improvements.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project activities in areas with varying security contexts. Construction activities in towns and communities require security planning. Labor influx for construction may create security concerns. Project includes measures to address SEA/SH risks through contractor Code of Conduct, training, and GRM. Procurement and implementation support missions require security awareness. Project design minimizes security risks by focusing on established towns and communities with government presence. Coordination with local authorities and security forces planned.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes - Land acquisition and involuntary resettlement risks identified. Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) prepared and disclosed. Sub-transmission and distribution lines may require land acquisition and relocation of structures. Project design aims to minimize impacts by routing lines along existing public roads and avoiding ecologically sensitive areas where possible. Poles have small footprints (≤1m diameter) to minimize land requirements. Solar PV installation in Moyamba proposed for unused 500-acre site to avoid displacement. Mini-grid sites selected considering land availability as criterion. Site-specific Resettlement Action Plans (RAPs) to be prepared during implementation based on detailed surveys and designs. Adequate budget to be provided for RAP implementation and compensation payments. Waterloo distribution network expansion is largest activity (one-third of project cost) with ESIA already prepared under ESURP. Potential impacts from 2017 landslides and floods in western areas inform design considerations.
